introduction.p: Chapter took the adding idea one dimension higher. A double integral adds over a region in the plane: f(x,y)\,dA. A triple integral adds over a solid: f(x,y,z)\,dV. The tiny pieces changed from rectangles to boxes. The story did not change: \text{value near a point}\times \text{tiny volume}.
The hard part is rarely the antiderivative. The hard part is describing the solid without counting too much or too little.

subsection.title: Skills: triple integrals in several coordinate systems

figure.caption: Choose coordinates by listening to the shape of the solid.

subsection.p:
The main volume elements are:
\begin{array}{c|c|c} \text{Coordinates} \amp \text{Conversion} \amp \text{Volume element} \\ \hline \text{rectangular} \amp (x,y,z) \amp dV=dx\,dy\,dz \\[2mm] \text{cylindrical} \amp x=r\cos\theta,\quad y=r\sin\theta,\quad z=z \amp dV=r\,dr\,d\theta\,dz \\[2mm] \text{spherical} \amp x=\rho\sin\phi\cos\theta,\quad y=\rho\sin\phi\sin\theta,\quad z=\rho\cos\phi \amp dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta \end{array}

The oriented version of rectangular volume is dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. For ordinary volume, mass, probability, and energy, we use positive volume: dV. For orientation-sensitive integrals later in the book, the wedge notation will remember the order of the directions.

Here are the main application formulas from the chapter.
\begin{array}{c|c} \text{Quantity} \amp \text{Triple integral} \\ \hline \text{volume of }E \amp \displaystyle \operatorname{Vol}(E)=\iiint_E 1\,dV \\[3mm] \text{mass from density }\delta \amp \displaystyle M=\iiint_E \delta\,dV \\[3mm] \text{center of mass} \amp \displaystyle (\bar x,\bar y,\bar z) = \left( \frac{\iiint_E x\delta\,dV}{M}, \frac{\iiint_E y\delta\,dV}{M}, \frac{\iiint_E z\delta\,dV}{M} \right) \\[5mm] \text{moment of inertia about the }z\text{-axis} \amp \displaystyle I_z=\iiint_E (x^2+y^2)\delta\,dV \\[4mm] \text{total charge from charge density }q \amp \displaystyle Q=\iiint_E q\,dV \\[3mm] \text{total energy from energy density }u \amp \displaystyle \mathcal E=\iiint_E u\,dV \\[3mm] \text{probability of a region }A \amp \displaystyle \operatorname{Prob}(A)=\iiint_A p\,dV \end{array}
A probability density must satisfy p\ge0 and \iiint_E p\,dV=1.

subsection.title: Worked review example: a wedge with density
subsection.p: A storage wedge has rectangular floor 0\le x\le2,\qquad 0\le y\le1, and slanted roof z=4-x-y. The floor is z=0. Its density is \delta(x,y,z)=1+z. Find the mass.

The solid is E=\{(x,y,z):0\le x\le2,\ 0\le y\le1,\ 0\le z\le4-x-y\}. So
M \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1\int_0^{4-x-y} (1+z)\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1 \left[ z+\frac12z^2 \right]_0^{4-x-y} \,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1 \left( (4-x-y)+\frac12(4-x-y)^2 \right)dy\,dx.
Expand the integrand: (4-x-y)+\frac12(4-x-y)^2 = 12-5x-5y+\frac12x^2+xy+\frac12y^2. Now integrate in y:
\int_0^1 \left( 12-5x-5y+\frac12x^2+xy+\frac12y^2 \right)dy \amp= \frac{29}{3}-\frac92x+\frac12x^2.
Then
M \amp= \int_0^2 \left( \frac{29}{3}-\frac92x+\frac12x^2 \right)dx \amp= \frac{58}{3}-9+\frac43 \amp= \frac{35}{3}.
Thus \boxed{ M=\frac{35}{3}. }
The bounds came from the geometry. The integrand came from the density. The calculation only came after both were clear.

subsection.title: Worked review example: a round tank in cylindrical coordinates
subsection.p: A round tank has radius 2. Its floor is z=0, and its roof height depends on distance from the central axis: z=3+\frac r2. The density is \delta(r,z)=1+r. Find the mass.

The solid is rotationally symmetric, so cylindrical coordinates fit: 0\le\theta\le2\pi, \qquad 0\le r\le2, \qquad 0\le z\le 3+\frac r2. Remember the volume factor: dV=r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta. So
M \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^2\int_0^{3+r/2} (1+r)r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta \amp= 2\pi\int_0^2 (1+r)r\left(3+\frac r2\right)dr.
Expand: (1+r)r\left(3+\frac r2\right) = 3r+\frac72r^2+\frac12r^3. Thus
M \amp= 2\pi\int_0^2 \left( 3r+\frac72r^2+\frac12r^3 \right)dr \amp= 2\pi \left[ \frac32r^2+\frac76r^3+\frac18r^4 \right]_0^2 \amp= 2\pi \left( 6+\frac{28}{3}+2 \right) \amp= \frac{104\pi}{3}.
So \boxed{ M=\frac{104\pi}{3}. }
The factor r is doing real work. Rings near r=2 contain more volume than rings near the central axis.

subsection.title: Worked review example: probability in a spherical cone
subsection.p: A random point is chosen in the solid inside the sphere \rho\le2 and inside the cone 0\le\phi\le\frac{\pi}{3}. The probability density is proportional to distance from the origin: p(\rho,\phi,\theta)=C\rho. Find C. Then find the probability that \rho\le1.
The ball of half the radius has much less than half the probability. Part of the reason is geometry: volume grows like \rho^2. Another part is density: this density also grows like \rho.

statement.p: What does dV mean in a triple integral?
Why is \iiint_E 1\,dV the volume of E?
What is the difference between dV and dx\wedge dy\wedge dz?
What does it mean for a solid to be z-simple?
Why does the innermost variable determine the coordinate shadow we should draw?
In cylindrical coordinates, why does dV contain the factor r?
In spherical coordinates, why does dV contain both \rho^2 \qquad\text{and}\qquad \sin\phi?
What angle does \phi measure in the spherical-coordinate convention used in this book?
Give one example of a solid that is better described in cylindrical coordinates than in rectangular coordinates.
Give one example of a solid that is better described in spherical coordinates than in cylindrical coordinates.
Why can the same solid have several correct iterated-integral descriptions?
Why does the center of mass require M\gt0?
What is the distance factor for moment of inertia about the z-axis?
What two conditions must a probability density satisfy?
In \mathbb R^n, why does scaling every coordinate by a scale volume by a^n?
Compute \int_0^2\int_0^1\int_0^3 (x+y+z)\,dz\,dy\,dx.
Find the volume of the solid E=\{(x,y,z):0\le x\le2,\ 0\le y\le x,\ 0\le z\le x+y\}.
Let E=\{(x,y,z):x\ge0,\ y\ge0,\ z\ge0,\ x+2y+3z\le6\}. Write an iterated integral for \iiint_E f(x,y,z)\,dV in the order dz\,dy\,dx.
For the same solid in , compute its volume.
Describe the solid represented by \int_0^4\int_0^{\sqrt{16-x^2}}\int_0^{x+y} f(x,y,z)\,dz\,dy\,dx. Use words and inequalities.
Rewrite \int_0^1\int_0^{1-x}\int_0^{1-x-y} f(x,y,z)\,dz\,dy\,dx with x as the innermost variable.
A box occupies 0\le x\le3,\qquad 0\le y\le2,\qquad 0\le z\le1. Its density is \delta(x,y,z)=2+x. Find its mass.
For the box in , find \bar x. Use symmetry to explain \bar y and \bar z.
A solid cylinder has radius a, height h, and constant density \delta_0. Use cylindrical coordinates to show that its moment of inertia about the central axis is I_z=\frac12Ma^2.
Find the volume inside the cylinder x^2+y^2\le9 and between the planes z=0 \qquad\text{and}\qquad z=4. Use cylindrical coordinates.
Find the volume inside the cone z=5-r above the floor z=0.
A pipe has inner radius 1, outer radius 3, and height 4. Its density is \delta(r)=\frac{1}{r}. Find its mass.
A round tank is inside r\le2 and below the slanted roof z=5+r\cos\theta. It is above the floor z=0. Set up and evaluate the volume integral.
Find the volume inside the sphere \rho\le3 and above the cone \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}.
Find the volume of the first octant part of the sphere \rho\le5.
Write a spherical-coordinate integral for the volume inside \rho\le4 and below the cone \phi=\frac{2\pi}{3}. Be careful: below the cone means farther down from the positive z-axis.
A ball of radius 2 has density \delta(\rho)=3+\rho. Find its mass.
A spherical shell satisfies 2\le\rho\le5. Its energy density is u(\rho)=\frac{12}{\rho^2}. Find the total energy.
A probability density on the unit cube is p(x,y,z)=C(x+y+z). Find C. Then find \operatorname{Prob}(z\le1/2).
A probability density on the ball \rho\le1 is p(\rho)=C. Find C. Then find the probability that \rho\le\frac12.
Decide which coordinate system you would use first: rectangular, cylindrical, or spherical. Give a reason. x^2+y^2\le4,\qquad 0\le z\le 7.
Decide which coordinate system you would use first: rectangular, cylindrical, or spherical. Give a reason. x^2+y^2+z^2\le9,\qquad z\ge0.
Decide which coordinate system you would use first: rectangular, cylindrical, or spherical. Give a reason. 0\le x\le2,\qquad 0\le y\le3,\qquad 0\le z\le 4-x.
The four-dimensional box B=[0,2]\times[0,1]\times[0,3]\times[0,4] has constant density 5. Find its total mass.
Find the volume of the four-dimensional ball of radius 3, using \operatorname{Vol}_4(B_4(R))=\frac{\pi^2}{2}R^4.
A point is chosen uniformly from the cube [-1,1]^6. Find the probability that every coordinate lies between -1/2 and 1/2.
Explain why the bounds 0\le\theta\le4\pi,\qquad 0\le r\le2,\qquad 0\le z\le1 describe the same cylinder twice.
Write the oriented volume form in rectangular coordinates, and then write its positive-volume version.

subsection.title: Project: comparing volume formulas numerically
subsection.p: A unit ball has volume \frac{4\pi}{3}. That formula is familiar, but this chapter gives several ways to approach the same number. The goal of this project is to compare three numerical estimates of the unit ball volume.
The unit ball is B=\{(x,y,z):x^2+y^2+z^2\le1\}.

subsubsection.title: Method 1. Counting small cubes
subsubsection.p: Divide the cube -1\le x\le1,\qquad -1\le y\le1,\qquad -1\le z\le1 into N^3 equal small cubes. Each small cube has side length \Delta x=\frac{2}{N} and volume \Delta V=\left(\frac{2}{N}\right)^3. Use the center of each small cube as a test point. Count how many centers satisfy x^2+y^2+z^2\le1. If that number is K_N, then \boxed{ V_N^{\mathrm{box}} = K_N\left(\frac{2}{N}\right)^3. }

subsubsection.title: Method 2. Cylindrical slices
subsubsection.p: In cylindrical coordinates, the ball is 0\le r\le1, \qquad -\sqrt{1-r^2}\le z\le \sqrt{1-r^2}, \qquad 0\le\theta\le2\pi. Its volume is V= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^1\int_{-\sqrt{1-r^2}}^{\sqrt{1-r^2}} r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta. After integrating in z and \theta, this becomes V=4\pi\int_0^1 r\sqrt{1-r^2}\,dr. Use a midpoint sum with r_i^*=\left(i-\frac12\right)\Delta r, \qquad \Delta r=\frac1N. Then \boxed{ V_N^{\mathrm{cyl}} = 4\pi\sum_{i=1}^N r_i^*\sqrt{1-(r_i^*)^2}\,\Delta r. }

subsubsection.title: Method 3. Spherical shells
subsubsection.p: In spherical coordinates, the unit ball is simply 0\le\rho\le1,\qquad 0\le\phi\le\pi,\qquad 0\le\theta\le2\pi. The volume is V= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\pi\int_0^1 \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta = 4\pi\int_0^1\rho^2\,d\rho. Use a midpoint sum with \rho_i^*=\left(i-\frac12\right)\Delta\rho, \qquad \Delta\rho=\frac1N. Then \boxed{ V_N^{\mathrm{sph}} = 4\pi\sum_{i=1}^N (\rho_i^*)^2\,\Delta\rho. }

subsubsection.title: Sample results
subsubsection.p:
The exact value is \frac{4\pi}{3}\approx 4.18879. Here are typical midpoint estimates.
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c} N \amp V_N^{\mathrm{box}} \amp V_N^{\mathrm{cyl}} \amp V_N^{\mathrm{sph}} \\ \hline 4 \amp 4.00000 \amp 4.34530 \amp 4.12334\\ 8 \amp 4.37500 \amp 4.24276 \amp 4.17243\\ 16 \amp 4.25000 \amp 4.20738 \amp 4.18470\\ 32 \amp 4.21289 \amp 4.19522 \amp 4.18777 \end{array}

The spherical estimate improves quickly because the spherical bounds match the ball. The cube-counting estimate also improves, but its boundary is rough: small cubes do not fit a sphere very naturally.

subsubsection.title: What to hand in
subsubsection.p: Write a short report with the following pieces.

li.p: A picture or description of the three methods.
A table of your estimates for at least four values of N.
The absolute error for each method: \left|V_N-\frac{4\pi}{3}\right|.
A paragraph explaining which method converges fastest in your computation.
A paragraph explaining why the coordinate system matters even though all three methods are estimating the same volume.

subsubsection.p: The last paragraph is the point of the project. A coordinate system is not just notation. It shapes the pieces being added.

subsection.title: Hook: coordinate changes need a systematic scale factor
subsection.p: Cylindrical coordinates gave dV=r\,dr\,d\theta\,dz. Spherical coordinates gave dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta. We found those factors by looking carefully at tiny coordinate boxes. That worked because cylinders and spheres have familiar geometry.

But there are many other coordinate changes. A square can be sheared into a parallelogram. A rectangle can be bent into a curved patch. A box can be stretched, tilted, or folded by a transformation. We need one rule that tells us the correct scale factor every time.

That rule is not new. It has been waiting since the determinant appeared as signed area and signed volume. In Chapter , the determinant returns as the automatic correction factor for changing variables. In the language of forms, it is what appears when dx\wedge dy \qquad\text{or}\qquad dx\wedge dy\wedge dz is pulled back through a coordinate map.
